description: A comprehensive beginner-friendly Python course covering 60+ foundational lessons with clear explanations and examples.

lessons.title: What is Python?
lessons.content: Python is a programming language used to write code that computers can understand. It is popular for being simple, readable, and powerful.

lessons.title: Printing Text
lessons.content: The print() function displays text or numbers on the screen. Text must be inside quotes.

lessons.title: Comments
lessons.content: Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores. They start with the # symbol.

lessons.title: Running Python Code
lessons.content: You can run Python code in an editor, a terminal, or an online environment. Code runs line by line, from top to bottom.

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.content: Variables are used to store information. You can give a name to a value using the = sign.

lessons.title: Rules for Variable Names
lessons.content: Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, cannot have spaces, and are case-sensitive.

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.content: Common data types in Python are int (numbers), float (decimals), str (text), and bool (True/False).

lessons.title: Printing Variables
lessons.content: You can print variables by including them in print().

lessons.title: Type Checking
lessons.content: Use the type() function to check what data type a variable has.

lessons.title: Numbers
lessons.content: Python can handle whole numbers (int) and decimals (float). You can perform arithmetic operations easily.

lessons.title: Addition and Subtraction
lessons.content: Use + for addition and - for subtraction.

lessons.title: Multiplication and Division
lessons.content: Use * for multiplication and / for division. Division always produces a float.

lessons.title: Exponentiation
lessons.content: Use ** to raise a number to a power.

lessons.title: Floor Division and Modulus
lessons.content: // divides and rounds down, % gives the remainder.

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.content: Strings are text in quotes. You can use single or double quotes.

lessons.title: Combining Strings
lessons.content: You can combine (concatenate) strings using +.

lessons.title: String Repetition
lessons.content: Use * to repeat a string multiple times.

lessons.title: String Indexing
lessons.content: You can access characters in a string using square brackets []. Indexing starts at 0.

lessons.title: String Slicing
lessons.content: You can get parts of a string using slicing: string[start:end].

lessons.title: String Methods
lessons.content: Strings have built-in methods to modify text like upper(), lower(), and replace().

lessons.title: Getting User Input
lessons.content: Use input() to get data from the user. It always returns a string.

lessons.title: Type Conversion
lessons.content: Convert data types using int(), float(), or str().

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.content: Booleans represent True or False values.

lessons.title: Comparison Operators
lessons.content: You can compare values with ==, !=, >, <, >=, and <=.

lessons.title: Logical Operators
lessons.content: and, or, and not combine or invert boolean values.

lessons.title: If Statements
lessons.content: If statements let you run code only when a condition is true.

lessons.title: Else Statements
lessons.content: Else runs when the if condition is false.

lessons.title: Elif Statements
lessons.content: Elif checks additional conditions when the first if is false.

lessons.title: Indentation
lessons.content: Python uses indentation (spaces) to mark code blocks instead of braces {}.

lessons.title: Nested If Statements
lessons.content: You can put if statements inside other if statements.

lessons.title: While Loops
lessons.content: A while loop repeats code as long as a condition is true.

lessons.title: For Loops
lessons.content: A for loop is used to loop through a sequence like a list, string, or range of numbers.

lessons.title: Range Function
lessons.content: The range() function creates a sequence of numbers, usually used in loops.

lessons.title: Breaking Out of Loops
lessons.content: Use break to stop a loop early when a condition is met.

lessons.title: Skipping with Continue
lessons.content: Use continue to skip to the next iteration of a loop.

lessons.title: Nested Loops
lessons.content: You can put one loop inside another loop. This is useful for working with grids or pairs of items.

lessons.title: Lists
lessons.content: Lists store multiple values in a single variable. Lists are ordered and can be changed.

lessons.title: Accessing List Elements
lessons.content: Access list elements using their index. Indexing starts at 0.

lessons.title: Changing List Elements
lessons.content: You can change an item in a list by assigning a new value to its index.

lessons.title: Looping Through Lists
lessons.content: Use a for loop to go through all items in a list.

lessons.title: List Methods
lessons.content: Lists have useful methods like append(), remove(), and sort().

lessons.title: List Slicing
lessons.content: You can get a part of a list using slicing: list[start:end].

lessons.title: Tuples
lessons.content: Tuples are like lists, but they cannot be changed (immutable). They use parentheses ().

lessons.title: Dictionaries
lessons.content: Dictionaries store key-value pairs. You can use keys to look up values.

lessons.title: Adding and Changing Dictionary Items
lessons.content: You can add or modify dictionary values using their keys.

lessons.title: Looping Through Dictionaries
lessons.content: You can loop through keys, values, or both in a dictionary.

lessons.title: Sets
lessons.content: Sets store unique items and are unordered. They are useful when you don’t want duplicates.

lessons.title: Adding and Removing from Sets
lessons.content: Use add() to add items and remove() to remove items from a set.

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.content: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks. Define them with def.

lessons.title: Function Parameters
lessons.content: Functions can take parameters, which are values you pass in.

lessons.title: Return Values
lessons.content: Functions can return values using the return keyword.

lessons.title: Default Parameters
lessons.content: You can give function parameters default values.

lessons.title: Scope of Variables
lessons.content: Variables created inside functions exist only inside them (local scope).
examples: # print(x) ❌ This would cause an error

lessons.title: Importing Modules
lessons.content: Modules are files that contain Python code you can reuse. Use import to include them.

lessons.title: Random Numbers
lessons.content: The random module can generate random numbers or choices.

lessons.title: Working with Strings and Numbers Together
lessons.content: You can’t add strings and numbers directly, but you can use commas in print() or convert using str().

lessons.title: File Reading
lessons.content: Use open() and read() to read text from a file.

lessons.title: File Writing
lessons.content: You can write to files using the 'w' mode with open(). Be careful—it replaces the file’s contents.

lessons.title: With Statement for Files
lessons.content: Using with automatically closes the file when done.

lessons.title: Putting It All Together
lessons.content: You can combine variables, loops, conditionals, and functions to make small programs.
